coredata.dc:title: Synthesis, solution and solid-state fluorescence of nitrogen self-doped carbon dots derived from Chlorella pyrenoidosa
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Dandan
Lyu, Yinong
Gao, Ying
Lin, Yunhao
Zhang, Xitong
Pan, Yueshen
Zhu, Yuezhao
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we report a simple and green approach for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from Chlorella pyrenoidosa, hereafter referred to as CP-CDs, via microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization without any additives. The effects of temperature (170–210 °C) and time (1–3 h) on the structure, functional groups, optical properties and formation route of the CP-CDs were assessed in detail. FTIR, XPS, and XRD results showed that high temperatures promoted the nitrogen doping level, resulting in good fluorescence intensity and high quantum yields (QYs). As the reaction temperature increased from 170 °C to 210 °C, the amount of nitrogen doped into the CP-CDs increased significantly from 4.1% to 11.9% and the absolute QY improved from 1.79% to 2.31%. The CP-CDs solutions exhibited excitation-dependent blue fluorescence from 360 to 527 nm with the excitation from 280 to 460 nm. The optimal preparation temperature and time for CP-CDs aqueous solution were 210 °C and 2 h, respectively. The solid-state CP-CDs without any further processing exhibited self-quenching resistant performance. The obtained CP-CDs as fluorescence inks could be used in security printing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chlorella pyrenoidosa
carbon dots
hydrothermal carbonization
solid-state fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of reaction temperature and time on the product yield
Structural and chemical characteristics of CP-CDs
Photophysical properties of CP-CDs in aqueous solutions and solid state
Possible formation route of the CP-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20201365 ); Aero-engine Thermal Environment and Structure Key Laboratory of Ministry of Industry and Information Technology ( CEPE2019008 ); Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Process Enhancement and New Energy Equipment Technology ; the Top-notch Academic Program Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions ; State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering ( KL20-06 ); Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China ( 19KJB610001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (Inset: HRTEM image and size distribution diagram); (b) AFM image (Inset: height profile along the line); (c) XRD patterns of CP-CDs; (d) FTIR spectra of the CP and CP-CDs.
(a) XPS full-scan survey spectra of CP-CDs prepared at 170 °C and 210 °C (b) High resolution XPS of C1s (c) High resolution XPS of N1s (c) High resolution XPS of O1s.
UV-Vis spectra of CP-CDs at different reaction temperatures.

PL emission spectra of (a) CP-CDs-170 °C (b) CP-CDs-190 °C and (c) CP-CDs-210 °C at 280–460 nm excitation wavelength; (d) the strongest PL spectra of CP-CDs prepared at 170–210 °C (the optimal excitation wavelength of CP-CDs-170 °C is 280 nm, and that of other samples is 340 nm); (e) the strongest PL spectra of CP-CDs at 210 °C for 1–3 h; (f) CP-CDs solutions under natural and UV light.
Solid-state CP-CDs under (a) natural light and (b) 365 nm UV light.

PL emission spectra of solid-state CP-CDs prepared at (a) 170 °C, (b) 180 °C, (c) 190 °C, (d) 200 °C, (e) 210 °C at 260–400 nm excitation wavelength; (f) the strongest PL spectra of CP-CDs prepared at 170–210 °C (the optimal excitation wavelength is 340 nm).
The images of the printed letters under natural light (a) and 365 nm UV light when irradiated for different time (b-d).
A predicted formation route of CP-CDs.
Proximate and component analyses (wt%) of Chlorella pyrenoidosa.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ad: air dry basis; A: Ash; FC: Fixed carbon; V: Volatile; M: Moisture.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Atomic concentrations of C, N, and O and their chemical states in the CP-CDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: N1: Pyridinic N; N2: Amine N; N3: Pyrrolic N; N4: Quaternary N.

head.ce:title: Synthesis, solution and solid-state fluorescence of nitrogen self-doped carbon dots derived from Chlorella pyrenoidosa

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we report a simple and green approach for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from Chlorella pyrenoidosa, hereafter referred to as CP-CDs, via microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization without any additives. The effects of temperature (170–210 °C) and time (1–3 h) on the structure, functional groups, optical properties and formation route of the CP-CDs were assessed in detail. FTIR, XPS, and XRD results showed that high temperatures promoted the nitrogen doping level, resulting in good fluorescence intensity and high quantum yields (QYs). As the reaction temperature increased from 170 °C to 210 °C, the amount of nitrogen doped into the CP-CDs increased significantly from 4.1% to 11.9% and the absolute QY improved from 1.79% to 2.31%. The CP-CDs solutions exhibited excitation-dependent blue fluorescence from 360 to 527 nm with the excitation from 280 to 460 nm. The optimal preparation temperature and time for CP-CDs aqueous solution were 210 °C and 2 h, respectively. The solid-state CP-CDs without any further processing exhibited self-quenching resistant performance. The obtained CP-CDs as fluorescence inks could be used in security printing.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomass energy is a type of renewable energy with abundant usable quantity. The photosynthesis of green plants can synthesize carbon dioxide and water into biomass, and the utilization of biomass energy generates carbon dioxide and water; these processes represent the carbon dioxide cycle. Therefore, increasing the utilization of biomass energy can effectively reduce carbon dioxide emissions and the greenhouse effect. Algae, as a third-generation biomass energy source, has received wide attention for its potential applications [1]. Compared with traditional agricultural and forestry biomass, algae have the advantages of high photosynthesis efficiency, short growth cycle, strong environmental adaptability, wide distribution, no land occupation, and low production cost. The transformation and utilization of algae, especially in the fields of bio-oil [2–4], biopolymer [5], bio-char [6], and biogas and bioethanol [7], have long aroused the interest of researchers worldwide. Algae has recently been employed as a green precursor to prepare carbon dots (CDs) and, thus, represent a new direction in the field of nanomaterial research.

CDs are an emerging nanomaterial with unique optical properties and potential application value. Since their accidental discovery by Xu in 2004 [8], these materials have quickly attracted the attention of researchers on a global scale. Several precursors, such as citric acid [9] and amino acids [10,11], obtained from various types of biomass, including vegetables [12], fruits [13,14], and even wastes [15–17], have been employed for the synthesis of CDs. Biomass is a green and environment-friendly raw material. The CDs prepared from biomass are called green CDs and have low toxicity and good biocompatibility. In addition, the heteroatoms in the biomass can be self-doped to avoid the complicated passivation process; self-doping also improves the optical properties of the CDs [18]. As a CDs precursor, biomass can replace expensive chemical reagents and improve the economics of CDs synthesis. In this sense, the advantages of biomass-based CDs are quite notable.

Hydrothermal carbonization is a simple and low-cost green method for the synthesis of biomass-derived CDs. Compared with traditional hydrothermal technology, microwave-assisted hydrothermal technology provides better heating efficiency and more uniform temperatures, thereby greatly reducing the reaction time. More importantly, the latest comparative experiments prove that the non-thermal effect of microwaves could lead to stronger luminescence and red-shifts in absorption and emission[19].

Studies have proved that carbon dots can be synthesized from some microalgae and have a variety of applications. Godavarthi et al. prepared N-doped CDs from the waste seaweed Sargassum fluitans via the hydrothermal method for DNA detection and revealed that amino acids act as nitrogen sources [20]. Ramanan et al. developed a rapid and simple microwave thermolysis method to produce fluorescent CDs with strong luminescence from eutrophic algal blooms for in vitro imaging [21]. Dou et al. synthesized cubic carbon quantum dots via the simple hydrothermal conversion of Gelidium amansii powders and used the resultant products as light-harvesters for mesoscopic solar cells [22]. Li et al. obtained nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs from the green alga Dunaliella salina via a one-step hydrothermal method and found that N/S-CDs are suitable for algal bio-imaging, fluorescence detection of Fe(III), and visible light-induced photodegradation of methylene blue and methyl violet [23]. By comparison, details of the effects of reaction conditions, such as temperature and reaction time, on the structures and properties CDs have rarely been reported. In fact, the structures and properties of CDs determine their applications, and the reaction temperature is a key factor affecting the hydrothermal preparation of CDs. However, to the best of our knowledge, the optimization of CDs synthesis derived from Chlorella pyrenoidosa via microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization (MHTC) has not yet been achieved.

Our previous work revealed that CDs synthesized from protein-rich biomass have excellent absolute quantum yields (QYs) and photoluminescence properties [24]. The crude protein content of C. pyrenoidosa is approximately 56.7%; thus, this species may be an ideal precursor for the preparation of CDs. In this work, we synthesized an excitation-dependent nitrogen-doped CDs by using C. pyrenoidosa via a simple MHTC.

The temperature range of this study is 170–210 °C according to the literature research above. Under a 365 nm UV lamp, the aqueous solution emits blue fluorescence, while the solid state emits bluish-white fluorescence. The optical properties of the C. pyrenoidosa-derived CDs (CP-CDs) are greatly affected by the hydrothermal temperature and time. The effect of temperature on nitrogen self-doping is also studied. The optimal synthesis conditions are discussed and the possible formation mechanism of the CP-CDs is proposed. Finally, the potential application of the obtained CP-CDs as security ink is explored.

C. pyrenoidosa powder was purchased from Wudi Lvqi Bioengineering Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). Powders of uniform particle diameter were dried without further processing. The proximate and component analyses (wt%) of C. pyrenoidosa are given in Table 1. Dialysis bags with a molecular weight of 1000 Da were obtained from Nanjing Wanqing Chemical Co., Ltd. Water was deionized and purified by the Smart-Q DI water purification system, and deionized (DI) water was used throughout this work.

The CP-CDs were synthesized by MHTC. In brief, 1 g of C. pyrenoidosa powder was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water, and the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined reaction vessel. A magnetic stir bar was placed in the reaction vessel, and the vessel was sealed and placed in a microwave workstation (maximum power, 1000 W; XO-SM100; Nanjing Xianou, China). The microwave power and magnetic stirring speed were set to 500 W and 200 rpm, respectively. The mixture was heated to reach a preset temperature (range, 170–210 °C) and maintained for 1–3 h. After a specified retention time, the reaction vessel was opened to cool to room temperature. The suspension was then ultracentrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 6 min to obtain the supernatant. The supernatant containing CP-CDs was collected and dialyzed in dialysis bags for 48 h to remove low-molecular weight compounds. The deionized water used for dialysis was changed every 4 h. After dialysis, the CP-CDs suspension was obtained. CP-CDs powders were collected by lyophilization of the suspension for 48 h. CP-CDs prepared at a specific temperature (e.g., 170 °C) were referred to as CP-CDs-<temperature>(e.g., CP-CDs-170). The solid residue was dried at 105 °C for 24 h, weighed, and stored for further experiments. Each experiment was repeated at least three times.

The yield distribution of solid- and gas–liquid-phase products was obtained from the mean values recorded from repeated experiments to analyze the influence of reaction temperature on the extent of reaction. Because the generation rate of gas-phase products under low-temperature hydrothermal conditions is very low, even nearly negligible, the distribution of the liquid-phase yield as considered approximately equal to the distribution of the total gas–liquid yield. The yields of solid- and gas–liquid-phase products were calculated according to the following equations:
(1)
Y
s
=
m
s
/
m ×
100 %
(2)
Y
L
= 100 %
−
Y
s
where Ys is the solid-phase product yield, YL is the liquid-phase yield, ms is the mass of the solid residue, and m is the mass of feedstock.

The morphology of the CDs was investigated using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEM-2100F), and the height and diameter of the CDs were observed by an atomic force microscope (AFM, Horiba, SmartSPM). The crystalline structure of the CDs was studied by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, SMARTLAB 9KW) using Cu-Kα radiation in the 2θ range of 10–90° and a step size of 0.002°. The surface functionalization of the CDs was characterized via Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy experiments on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer with KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 instrument using monochromatic Al Kα radiation; the building energy step size was set to 0.100 eV. UV-Vis absorption spectra were obtained at room temperature by using a Lambda 750 s spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra measurements were conducted on a PerkinElmer FL6500 spectrometer equipped with a Xe lamp at ambient conditions. Absolute photoluminescence QYs were measured on an Edinburgh FLS100 steady/transient state fluorescence spectrometer with a Xe lamp as the light source and a photomultiplier tube sphere as the EML detector light source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of reaction temperature and time on the product yield
ce:section.ce:para: The reaction temperature and time have a great influence on the product yield distribution, according to Eqs. (1) and (2). In the range of reaction temperature and time employed in this experiments, the higher the temperature and the longer the reaction time, the higher liquid phase yield. When the temperature rised from 170 °C to 210 °C (the time was 1 h), the liquid phase yield increased from 61% to 80%, and the corresponding solid phase yield decreased from 39% to 20%. When the reaction time increased from 1 h to 3 h (the temperature was 200 °C), the liquid phase yield increased from 76% to 89%, while the corresponding solid phase yield decreased from 24% to 11%. During MHTC, the raw materials are continuously broken down first into large molecules and then into low-molecular weight compounds. Low-molecular weight compounds obtained by hydrolysis are dissolved in the aqueous solution. In addition, complex polymerization, cross-linking, carbonization, and other reactions occurring between the intermediate products produce CDs dispersed in the liquid phase. The liquid-phase yield is one of the most important factors affecting the formation and concentration of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and chemical characteristics of CP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM and AFM measurements were conducted to observe the morphology of the CP-CDs. The CP-CDs were synthesized under typical conditions of 170 °C and 1 h. As shown in the TEM image in Fig. 1a, the CP-CD particles were round and uniformly dispersed and had diameters ranging from 3 nm to 6.5 nm. According to the size-distribution histogram shown in the inset in Fig. 1a, the average size of the CP-CDs could be estimated to be 4.5 nm. A typical HR-TEM image of the CP-CDs (Fig. 1a, inset) revealed that the prepared CP-CDs have legible lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, which corresponds to the (100) diffraction facet of graphite. This finding demonstrates that the CP-CDs have a carbon core similar to that of graphite, which is mostly composed of sp2 hybridized carbons[25]. The representative AFM image shown in Fig. 1b illustrates that the CP-CDs are well-dispersed in ethanol. The height of the CP-CDs determined from cross-sectional topography (Fig. 1b, inset) was approximately 5 nm. TEM and AFM images confirm that the as-prepared CP-CDs have an approximate spherical structure.

XRD characterization is an important method used to study the microstructure of matter. Fig. 1c shows a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.5° and an inconspicuous peak at approximately 2θ = 43°; these peaks respectively correspond to the (002) and (100) planes of the graphite-like structure and indicate that the CP-CDs have highly disordered carbon structures and poor crystallinity. The diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.5° changes with the preparation temperature of the samples. The factors that cause the broadening of the diffraction peak come from the instrument and the sample. The broadening caused by the sample also includes the effect of the size of the crystal block, the inhomogeneous strain and the stacking dislocation.

FTIR spectroscopy was performed to investigate the surface functional groups of the raw material CP and its derived CP-CDs. Aa shown in Fig. 1d, characteristic peaks and their intensity changed with temperatures and time. The characteristic peaks at 1642 cm−1, 1530 cm−1 and 1456 cm−1 were attributed to the CO stretching (amide I), N–H stretching (amide II) and C–N stretching (amide III), respectively [24]. These characteristic peaks indicated that both the raw material and the CP-CDs contain peptide bonds[26]. The above three peaks displayed the highest intensity for CP-CDs-180, and then decreased with the increasing temperature, which proved that peptide bonds are temperature sensitive. In addition, N-containing functional groups including C–N and N–H demonstrated the successful self-doping of nitrogen atoms in the CDs. The wide absorption band at approximately 3280 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H or N–H bonds. Experimental results showed that the as-synthesized CP-CDs maintain good dispersion in water for an extended period of time because of their hydrophilic functional groups, such as O–H. The intensity of the band at 3280 cm−1 was strongest in CP-CDs-180 and then decreased gradually from CP-CDs-190 to CP-CDs-210. The peaks at 2935 and 1395 cm−1 were attributed to C–H stretching and deformation stretching vibrations, respectively, and unremarkably affected by the temperature. The peaks at 1078 and 1027 cm−1 were due to C–O stretching vibrations. The peak at 1078 cm−1 varied slightly with increasing temperature, while the sharp peak at 1027 cm−1 changed markedly from 170 °C to 200 °C. At 210 °C, the peak at 1027 cm−1 disappeared; this finding indicates that this functional group is sensitive to temperature and may be destroyed at high temperatures. The reaction time also significantly affected the change of functional groups. The characteristic peaks of O–H and C–O became weaker with reaction time, suggesting that the longer reaction time helps the reactions of dehydration and decarboxylation, which results in the decrease of oxygen-containing functional groups. The peak of amide I (CO) displayed higher intensity with longer reaction time, showing an opposite trend. The intensity of C–H (2935 cm−1), N–H (1530 cm−1) and C–N (1456 cm−1) was higher when the reaction time was 2 h. In general, the intensity of nearly all peaks observed in the spectra was strongest at 180 °C, thereby demonstrating that an MHTC temperature of 180 °C may contribute to the retention of functional groups on the surface of the CP-CDs. These results suggest that temperature plays a crucial role in the formation of functional groups on the surface of the CP-CDs. A lower temperature results in an incomplete reaction, while excessive temperatures destroy the functional groups of the CP-CDs. Meanwhile, 1–2 h is sufficient for the preparation of CP-CDs by MHTC, which is significantly shorter than that of hydrothermal carbonization. Therefore, seeking a suitable reaction temperature and time for the formation of functional groups on the surface of CDs is a meaningful endeavor.

The presence of C, N, and O and their valence states was confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2 illustrates the XPS full-scan and high-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra of the CP-CDs prepared at 170 and 210 °C, respectively. The elemental chemical states and their relative amounts were then analyzed. Fig. 2a shows three obvious peaks at 284.8, 399.8, and 531.2 eV, which could be attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. C and O were the dominant elements, and the relative contents of these elements varied as shown in Table 2. As the temperature increased from 170 °C to 210 °C, the atomic content of C increased from 61.6% to 69.2%, that of N increased significantly from 4.1% to 11.9%, but that of O decreased obviously from 34.3% to 18.9%. The higher the reaction temperature, the deeper the degree of graphitization, the higher carbon content of the CP-CDs, and the lower their oxygen content. As the temperature increases, volatiles are continuously precipitated out, and the combination of hydroxide radicals and H+ in water reduces the oxygen content of the CP-CDs. Most of the oxygen atoms are removed by dehydration and decarboxylation reactions. More importantly, appropriate increases in temperature contribute to the nitrogen self-doping of the CP-CDs when all other reaction conditions are held constant. The nitrogen content of the CP-CDs (i.e., 11.9%) was higher than those found by Godavarthi et al. (9.85%) [20] and Li et al. (5.0%) [23], both of whom obtained CDs derived from alga via one-step hydrothermal carbonization. The above results demonstrate the superiority of C. pyrenoidosa as CD precursors and the effectiveness of MHTC for incorporating nitrogen into the CP-CDs.

The deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, and O1s revealed that CP-CDs prepared at different temperatures contain the same chemical bonds. The high-resolution XPS of C1s was deconvoluted, as shown in Fig. 2b, to reveal five peaks at 284.3, 284.8–284.9, 286.2, 287.3–287.4, and 288.1–288.2 eV, which correspond to CC, C–C, C–N, C–O, and CO, respectively[24]. As the temperature rose from 170 °C to 210 °C, the content of CO increased from 3.75% to 17.50%. This significant increase may be attributed to the breakage of glycosidic bonds in oligosaccharides and polysaccharides and the release of more carbonyl groups with increasing temperature. The content of C–N was as high as 30.60% at 210 °C, thus suggesting that nitrogen was doped into the carbon framework. The percentage of sp2 hybrid carbon atoms (CC) in the CP-CDs was calculated from the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s and found to be 9.63–26.38%. This finding further demonstrating the low graphitization of the CP-CDs. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s was deconvoluted into four peaks at 398.5–398.6, 399.6–399.7, 400.6, and 401.4 eV, as shown in Fig. 2c; these peaks were assigned to four different chemical states of nitrogen, namely, pyridinic N, amine N, pyrrolic N, and quaternary N, respectively [27]. The relative content of amine N decreased from 68.08% to 65.51% with increasing temperature, owing to that amine nitrogen is easily converted to inorganic ammonium at high temperature. The relative content of pyrrolic N increased from 15.73% to 20.24% as the temperature increased from 170 °C to 210 °C. The high resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2d) was deconvoluted into two peaks attributed to CO (531.0–531.3 eV) and C–O (532.3–532.6 eV) [24]. The increase in C–O content as the temperature increased could be associated with the ring-opening reaction of pyran at high temperatures to form low-molecular weight compounds, such as glycolaldehyde and 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (5-HMF). Carbonyl compounds could be reduced to alcohol compounds, which, in turn, could be dehydrated to ether compounds, which may promote a decrease in carbonyl content. Several of these compounds could be transformed into each other to achieve dynamic equilibrium under certain conditions. Taken together, the XPS results reveal that chemical bonds or functional groups are strongly influenced by the treatment temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical properties of CP-CDs in aqueous solutions and solid state
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 depicts the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CP-CDs prepared at 170–210 °C. The CP-CDs had strong absorption in the UV region and weak absorption in the visible region. Two obvious absorption peaks at approximately 220 and 280 nm and a fairly week absorption peak at 330 nm could be observed in the spectra obtained. The absorption peaks at 220 and 280 nm are due to the π→π* transition of CC bonds, which originates from conjugated sp2 region in the graphite-like core [28,29]. The evolution of the CC bond is related to the aromatization of the nanoparticle core [30]. The absorption peak at 330 nm could be assigned to the n→π* transition of CO or other surface groups. According to previous research, the n→π* transition may be related to surface defects and nitrogen doping could produce additional surface defects and reduce the bandgap [24]. The absorbance of CP-CDs varied with the temperature at different UV-Vis absorption peaks. According to the complete UV–vis absorption spectra of the CP-CDs, the spectra of CP-CDs synthesized at 180 and 190 °C were highly coincident, thus indicating that the structure of the CDs does not remarkably change over this temperature range. At 280 nm, the absorption intensity (Fig. 3, inset) first increased and then decreased with the temperature; here, the influence of temperature on the absorbance of the CP-CDs was more obvious, and CP-CDs-200 revealed the strongest absorbance among the samples obtained. The absorption peak at 340 nm was moderate, and the effect of temperature at this wavelength was consistent with that at 280 nm. In general, temperature had a significant influence on the absorbance of the CP-CDs in the UV light region, which means this factor exerts a great influence on the structure of the CP-CDs.

The PL spectra of the CDs were measured over the excitation wavelength range of 280–460 nm at intervals of 20 nm, as shown in Fig. 4a–c. The PL spectra of CP-CDs-170 (Fig. 4a), CP-CDs-190 (Fig. 4b), and CP-CDs-210 (Fig. 4c) exhibited excitation-dependent emission and a red-shift with increasing excitation wavelength. The maximum PL emission peak of CP-CDs-170 shifted from 360 nm to 527 nm, that of CP-CDs-190 shifted from 373 nm to 527 nm, and that of CP-CDs-210 shifted from approximately 425 nm to 527 nm as the excitation wavelength increased from 280 nm to 460 nm. We observed that the PL intensity first increased and then decreased remarkably with increasing excitation wavelength from 280 nm to 460 nm and the optimal excitation wavelength of all samples except CP-CDs-170 was 340 nm. Interestingly, the PL peak intensity of CP-CDs-170 excited at 280 nm was much higher than the intensities obtained at other wavelengths. N-doping can increase the energy level and reduce the band gap, thus explaining the excitation-dependence of the CDs [29]. The surface-state luminescence mechanism may also contribute to this phenomenon. The luminescence of the CDs originates from differences in the latter’s surface states, each of which can be excited by a specific range of wavelengths [31]. Finally, CDs with a large number of functional groups may exhibit excitation-dependent characteristics. The change in temperature leads to variations in functional groups and the formation of more surface defects, thereby narrowing the energy levels [32]. The FTIR and XPS results obtained earlier confirm that the functional groups of the CP-CDs change with the temperature. Fig. 4d presents the PL spectra of CP-CDs synthesized at 170 – 210 °C at the respective optimal excitation wavelengths. The PL emission peaks displayed a red-shift from 360 nm to 425 nm as the temperature of the sample preparation increased from 170 °C to 210 °C, including a slight red-shift from 422 nm to 425 nm and a gradual increase in peak intensity as the temperature of the sample preparation rose from 180 °C to 210 °C. Therefore, the PL emission of CP-CDs is not only excitation-dependent but also related with the temperature at which the sample prepared. The optimal excitation wavelength of the CP-CDs-170 was unique 280 nm, while that of the CP-CDs obtained at 180 – 210 °C red-shifted to 340 nm. In this work, higher temperatures increase the concentration of the CDs solution, which results in a red-shift of the optimal excitation wavelength. Lai et al. demonstrated that the optimal excitation wavelength could be red-shifted as the concentration increases; in fact, this group found that the optimal excitation wavelength of low-concentration solutions is 280 nm while that of high-concentration solutions is approximately 330 nm [33]. Many researchers have proved that the surface functional groups of the CDs exert a remarkable influence on their electronic structures, electronic transitions, and fluorescence properties. We analyzed the relationship between the origin of the fluorescence of the CP-CDs and their functional groups and electronic states. The optimal excitation wavelength of 280 nm is due to the nO2p→σ* transition, which is related to oxygen-containing functional groups in the sp3 domain. The FTIR results indicated that the CP-CDs are composed of CO, C–O, and O–H bonds. The optimal excitation wavelength of 340 nm may be ascribed to the π→π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain [34], which is also confirmed by the XPS results. According to Fig. 4e, it can be concluded that 2 h is appropriate for the preparation of CP-CDs by MHTC in consideration of PL intensity and economics. In Fig. 4f, we can visually observe the color change of CP-CDs solutions under natural light and the intensity change of the blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. The color of CP-CDs solution becomes darker with the increase of preparation temperatures.

The absolute QYs of CP-CDs-170, CP-CDs-190, and CP-CDs-210 without any modification were 1.79 ± 0.026%, 1.81 ± 0.025%, and 2.31 ± 0.036%, respectively. The absolute QYs were obtained with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm and the scanning range of emission wavelength of 320–650 nm. Several factors may account for the phenomenon. Firstly, the higher the temperature, the higher the concentration of CP-CDs. Secondly, the carbonization degree of the CP-CDs also increased with the temperature. Moreover, higher temperatures favor the generation of N­doped aromatic rings, and heteroatom doping can change the structure and properties of the CDs [35]. According to a previous study [36], N-doping can induce new energy levels between the band gaps, produce more surface defects, and enhance the fluorescence of CDs. The luminescence dependence in the blue region (440–460 nm) may be mainly attributed to increases in N-doping, particularly because N atoms have a radius similar to that of C and a lone pair of electrons with high affinity [37]. However, Li et al. verified that the N-containing by-product could also be physically adsorbed on the surface of the dialyzed CDs, thus affecting the fluorescence properties [38]. In short, nitrogen affects the fluorescence and quantum yield of CDs in different forms.

Solid-state CP-CDs were obtained by lyophilization of the CP-CDs aqueous solutions, exhibiting special fluorescence characteristics. This phenomenon of direct solid-state luminescence is very rare in biomass-derived CDs without any modification. In Fig. 5a, the color of the solid-state CP-CDs under natural light gradually changed from light brown to brown with the increase of the preparation temperature, which is consistent with the color change of the CP-CDs solutions in Fig. 4f. The color of the solid-state CP-CDs once again reflected the degree of carbonization of the CP-CDs deepens as the temperature rises. Under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 5b), solid-state CP-CDs of 170 °C emitted bluish-white fluorescence, that of 180 °C emitted bluish-gray fluorescence, that of 190 °C emitted gray fluorescence, while that 200 °C and 210 °C showed almost no fluorescence. The PL emission spectra of all solid-state CP-CDs (prepared at 170–210 °C) showed similar characteristics ( Fig. 6a-e). When the excitation wavelength increased from 260 to 400 nm, all the fluorescence spectra exhibited wide peaks from about 400–700 nm, and the peak position changed little. The emission peak of CP-CDs prepared at 170–190 °C was at about 510 nm, and that of CP-CDs prepared at 200–210 °C was at about 550 nm. The optimal excitation wavelength of the above solid-state CP-CDs was 340 nm, and the corresponding emission peak intensity decreased continuously with the increase of the preparation temperature, and the emission peaks of CP-CDs prepared at 200–210 °C showed a red shift and the intensity decreased significantly (Fig. 6f). This is consistent with the luminescence changes observed in Fig. 5b. Fig. 6f also showed that a wide emission peak actually contained several small peaks. The PL characteristics of CP-CDs in solid state are quite different from those of CP-CDs in liquid state. Obviously, the PL intensity of solid-state CP-CDs prepared from low temperature is stronger, which is contrary to the trend of CP-CDs in liquid state. The fluorescence color of solid-state CP-CDs is also different from that of liquid, which shows that the source of fluorescence is different. The fluorescence of solid-state CP-CDs may originate from the fluorophore on the surface [39]. Changes in the distance of the fluorophore on the surface of the CDs leads to changes in the electron distribution on the surface of the CDs, thus resulting in changes in band gaps and Förster resonance energy transfer [40]. Here, the CP-CDs powder prepared at 170–190 °C can maintain its original loose state in the air, while that prepared at 200–210 °C quickly absorbs moisture and becomes sticky when exposed to air. Therefore, we believe that as the preparation temperature increases, the aggregation degree of lyophilized CP-CDs becomes deeper and deeper, and the excessive aggregation leads to the weakening and even quenching of the fluorescence. The solid-state CP-CDs in this work can emit bright fluorescence without adding any additional matrix, which is unique and also worthy of further study and exploration of the potential applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible formation route of the CP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Combining our results with the existing research, we propose a formation route for the CP-CDs and predict the influence of reaction temperature and time on this formation process. A schematic diagram of the formation of CP-CDs is displayed ( Scheme 1). In the process of MHTC, the main component of C. pyrenoidosa crude protein is hydrolyzed to produce amino acids, after which deamination and decarboxylation reactions occur to produce amines, acids, and other compounds [41]. The glycosidic bonds of cellulose and hemicellulose are hydrolyzed to produce oligosaccharides and glucose, as well as hydrocarbons and acid compounds. Glucose is dehydrated to form low-molecular weight compounds, such as 5-HMF and furfural [42]. High temperatures are conducive to the formation of low-molecular-weight heterocyclic compounds. Pyran is sensitive to heat and could be cyclized and aromatized after ring opening to form new carbocyclic or heterocyclic compounds, such as pyrrole, pyridine, and furan, in accordance with our findings from the high-resolution XPS of N1s in Fig. 2c. N- and O-heterocyclic compounds, such as pyridine, pyrrole, and their derivatives, are also formed through the Maillard reaction between amino groups in peptides and amino acids and carbonyl compounds; for example, reducing sugars could be produced from the hydrolysis of proteins and carbohydrates [43]. Under the action of microwaves, low-molecular weight compounds, heterocyclic compounds, and intermediate products produced by the above reactions may undergo complex reactions, such as crosslinking, polycondensation, and carbonization, to form polymers and CDs. Crystal nuclei are formed after the critical nucleation concentration was reached. The crystal nuclei continue to grow through diffusion. Compounds in the liquid phase are continuously adsorbed and diffused to the surface of the crystal nucleus and finally form stable functional groups. The degree of graphitization of CP-CDs and the content of surface functional groups are closely related to the reaction conditions from the above comparative experimental results. Higher temperature (210 °C) and longer time (3 h) can improve the fluorescence performance of CP-CDs in aqueous solution. However, for solid-state CP-CDs, the surface fluorescence molecules are sensitive to temperature, so a lower synthesis temperature (170 °C) is beneficial to their luminescence. The luminescence characteristics of CP-CDs can be controlled by adjusting the reaction conditions, which can provide a reference for designing synthesis conditions and obtaining ideal carbon dots.

In view of the excellent luminescence characteristics of the obtained CP-CDs, we use CP-CDs as fluorescent inks for anti-counterfeiting and information security. 20 mg of CP-CDS powder was dispersed in 4 mL of deionized water, and then 1 mL glycerin was added as the thickener. After thoroughly mixing, fluorescent ink was formed. The abbreviation of the school name (NJTECH) was printed on the non-fluorescent paper with a stamp using the as prepared fluorescent ink. Under natural light ( Fig. 7a), almost nothing was observed. Under the 365 nm UV light (Fig. 7b), the letters emit cyan fluorescence in dry state. Even when it was continuously exposured to the 365 nm UV light for 60–120 min (Fig. 7c-d), the fluorescence intensity did not decrease significantly with the naked eye, which proved that the fluorescent ink has a strong ability to resist photobleaching. Therefore, the obtained CP-CDs may be applied in security printing or anti-counterfeit label.

In summary, N-doped CP-CDs were synthesized from C. pyrenoidosa without any other reagents by microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization. Protein-rich C. pyrenoidosa provided natural carbon and nitrogen sources, and the MHTC method was proven to be a simple, facile, and effective way to produce the CP-CDs. The N-doped CP-CDs possess abundant functional groups, which are greatly affected by the reaction temperature and time. The aforementioned CP-CDs emits blue fluorescence in aqueous solution and the fluorescence intensity increases with increasing temperature. The QY is closely related to reaction temperature and N doping. The solid-state CP-CDs exhibit self-quenching-resistant fluorescence without any treatment and have the strongest fluorescence at 170 °C. The fluorescent color and PL mechanism of solid-state CP-CDs are completely different from those of liquid. Moreover, a possible formation mechanism of CP-CDs has been discussed to achieve better control of the reaction process and improve the performance of the CP-CDs. Finally, we successfully prepared fluorescent ink for stamps, indicating that CP-CDs can be processed and used in anti-counterfeiting.

Dandan Guo: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yinong Lyu: Methodology, Supervision. Ying Gao: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Yunhao Lin: Software, Validation, Investigation. Xitong Zhang: Visualization, Data curation. Yueshen Pan: Data curation, Investigation. Yuezhao Zhu: Project administration, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20201365); Aero-engine Thermal Environment and Structure Key Laboratory of Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (CEPE2019008); Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Process Enhancement and New Energy Equipment Technology; the Top-notch Academic Program Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions; State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering (KL20-06); Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (19KJB610001).
